reference: GATE CS — Theory of Computation / Computability

map.condition: Is the set of all strings over a finite alphabet countable or uncountable?
Is the power set of Sigma* (set of all languages) countable?

map.use: L \text{ is recursive} \iff L \text{ and } \overline{L} \text{ are both recursively enumerable}

map.condition: Reducing undecidable P2 to P3 — what can we conclude about P3?
Is the halting problem decidable for a given TM and input?
map.use: \text{HALT}_{TM} = \{\langle M,w\rangle \mid M \text{ halts on } w\} \text{ is undecidable}

formulas.name: Sigma* is Countably Infinite
formulas.latex: \Sigma^* \text{ is countably infinite for any finite } \Sigma \neq \emptyset
formulas.plain: Sigma* (set of all finite strings over finite Sigma) is countably infinite
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether the set of all strings over a finite alphabet is countable

terms.means: set of all finite strings over alphabet Sigma

formulas.trap: Students confuse Sigma* (countable) with 2^(Sigma*) (uncountable). Sigma* can be enumerated by listing strings in length-lexicographic order.

formulas.name: Power Set of Sigma* is Uncountable
formulas.plain: 2^(Sigma*), the set of all languages over Sigma, is uncountably infinite
formulas.whenToUse: When comparing the cardinality of the set of all languages vs. set of all strings

terms.means: power set of Sigma* = set of ALL languages over Sigma

formulas.trap: The set of all regular/context-free languages is countable (each is described by a finite grammar/regex), but the set of ALL languages is uncountable.

formulas.name: Cantor's Diagonal Argument
formulas.latex: \text{If } f: \mathbb{N} \to S \text{ for any set } S, \text{ and we can always find an element not in } f,\text{ then } S \text{ is uncountable}
formulas.plain: No onto mapping exists from N to 2^(Sigma*); used to prove uncountability
formulas.whenToUse: When proving a set cannot be put in one-to-one correspondence with natural numbers
formulas.trap: Cantor's argument only shows the set is uncountable; it does not identify which specific element is missing.

formulas.name: Set of RE Languages is Countable
formulas.latex: \text{Set of all RE languages over } \Sigma \text{ is countably infinite (each has a TM description)}
formulas.plain: Recursively enumerable languages are countable because each is described by a finite TM
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying whether the collection of RE languages is countable or not

terms.means: recursively enumerable (accepted by some Turing machine)

formulas.trap: The set of all TMs is countable (each TM has a finite encoding), so the set of languages accepted by TMs is also countable — but fewer than all languages.

formulas.name: Uncountably Many Non-RE Languages Exist
formulas.latex: |2^{\Sigma^*}| > |\{L \mid L \text{ is RE}\}| \Rightarrow \text{most languages are not RE}
formulas.plain: Since all languages are uncountable but RE languages are countable, most languages are not RE
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about the existence or density of non-RE or undecidable languages

terms.means: cardinality of set of all languages

formulas.trap: Every non-recursive language is not a subset of the RE languages; non-regular does not imply non-RE.

formulas.name: Recursive (Decidable) Language
formulas.latex: L \text{ is recursive (decidable)} \iff \exists \text{ TM } M: M \text{ halts on every input and accepts } L
formulas.plain: L is recursive if some TM always halts and decides membership in L
formulas.whenToUse: When determining if a language is decidable
formulas.trap: A decider always halts (accept OR reject). A TM that only halts on accepting inputs is a recognizer, not a decider.

formulas.name: Recursively Enumerable (RE) Language
formulas.latex: L \text{ is RE} \iff \exists \text{ TM } M: M \text{ accepts } w \in L \text{ (may loop on } w \notin L\text{)}
formulas.plain: L is RE if some TM accepts all strings in L (but may loop on strings not in L)
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying languages accepted by TMs that may not halt

terms.means: TM may loop forever on strings outside L

formulas.trap: RE does not mean decidable. Halting problem language is RE but not recursive.

formulas.name: Recursive iff Both L and L-complement are RE
formulas.plain: L is decidable if and only if both L and its complement are recursively enumerable
formulas.whenToUse: When given info about recognizability of L and complement(L) and asked about decidability
formulas.trap: If L is RE but complement(L) is NOT RE, then L is not recursive. The halting problem is exactly this case.

formulas.name: Language Class Containment
formulas.plain: Regular < CFL < Context-Sensitive < Recursive < RE < All Languages
formulas.whenToUse: When checking inclusion or decidability for various classes of languages
formulas.trap: RE is not equal to All Languages — there exist languages that are not RE (not recognizable by any TM).

formulas.name: Halting Problem (Undecidable)
formulas.latex: \text{HALT}_{TM} = \{\langle M,w \rangle \mid M \text{ halts on input } w\} \text{ is undecidable}
formulas.plain: HALT_TM = { <M,w> | TM M halts on input w } is undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about the decidability of whether a TM halts on a given input

terms.means: encoding of TM M and input string w

formulas.trap: HALT_TM is RE (we can simulate M and accept if it halts) but is NOT recursive (we cannot always detect loops).

formulas.name: Acceptance Problem A_TM (Undecidable)
formulas.latex: A_{TM} = \{\langle M,w \rangle \mid M \text{ accepts } w\} \text{ is undecidable}
formulas.plain: A_TM = { <M,w> | M accepts w } is undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: When asked whether a TM accepts a given input string
formulas.trap: A_TM is RE (simulate M on w and accept if M accepts) but undecidable (cannot decide rejection/looping).

formulas.name: Rice's Theorem
formulas.latex: \text{Every non-trivial semantic property of RE languages is undecidable}
formulas.plain: Any non-trivial property of the language recognized by a TM is undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: When asked if a property of a TM (based on what language it accepts) is decidable

terms.means: property that is true for some TMs and false for others
about the language accepted, not the TM description itself

formulas.trap: Rice's theorem applies to semantic (language) properties, NOT syntactic properties (e.g., 'TM has 5 states' IS decidable). Also, trivial properties (always true or always false) are decidable.

formulas.name: Post's Correspondence Problem (Undecidable)
formulas.latex: \text{PCP: Given pairs } (u_i, v_i), \exists \text{ sequence } i_1,\ldots,i_k \text{ s.t. } u_{i_1}\cdots u_{i_k} = v_{i_1}\cdots v_{i_k}? \text{ Undecidable.}
formulas.plain: PCP: Given pairs of strings, find an index sequence making top and bottom concatenations equal — undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: When asked about PCP or reductions from/to PCP

terms.means: pairs of strings (tiles) in the PCP instance

formulas.trap: PCP is undecidable but it is easy to construct valid instances with solutions. The decision problem is what is undecidable.

formulas.name: Many-One (Mapping) Reduction
formulas.latex: A \leq_m B \iff \exists \text{ computable } f: w \in A \iff f(w) \in B
formulas.plain: A reduces to B (A <=_m B) if there is a computable function f such that w in A iff f(w) in B
formulas.whenToUse: When proving a new problem is undecidable by reducing a known undecidable problem to it

terms.means: A is many-one reducible to B

formulas.trap: Direction matters: A <=_m B means B is at least as hard as A. To show B is undecidable, reduce a known undecidable A to B (not B to A).

formulas.name: Reduction: Decidability Transfer
formulas.latex: A \leq_m B \text{ and } B \text{ decidable} \Rightarrow A \text{ decidable}
formulas.plain: If A reduces to B and B is decidable, then A is decidable
formulas.whenToUse: When using reduction to prove decidability (contrapositive: if A undecidable then B undecidable)
formulas.trap: Contrapositive: if A is undecidable and A <=_m B, then B is undecidable. Do NOT conclude A is decidable just because B is (must have A <=_m B in the right direction).

formulas.name: Reduction: Undecidability Transfer
formulas.latex: A \leq_m B \text{ and } A \text{ undecidable} \Rightarrow B \text{ undecidable}
formulas.plain: If A is undecidable and A reduces to B, then B is undecidable
formulas.whenToUse: Standard method to prove a new problem B is undecidable

terms.means: problem whose undecidability we want to prove

formulas.trap: Reducing B to A (instead of A to B) does NOT prove B undecidable — it only shows A is at least as hard as B.

formulas.name: Reduction: RE Transfer
formulas.latex: A \leq_m B \text{ and } B \in \text{RE} \Rightarrow A \in \text{RE} \quad (\text{and contrap: } A \notin \text{RE} \Rightarrow B \notin \text{RE})
formulas.plain: If A reduces to B and B is RE, then A is RE; contrapositive: if A is not RE then B is not RE
formulas.whenToUse: When classifying RE / non-RE languages using reduction
formulas.trap: This holds for many-one reduction, not just Turing reduction. For co-RE: A <=_m B and B co-RE implies A co-RE.

formulas.name: Transitivity of Reduction
formulas.latex: A \leq_m B \text{ and } B \leq_m C \Rightarrow A \leq_m C
formulas.plain: Reduction is transitive: if A reduces to B and B reduces to C, then A reduces to C
formulas.whenToUse: When reasoning about chains of reductions across multiple problems
formulas.trap: Transitivity does not mean all three have the same decidability status — only that hardness is inherited upward in the chain.
